content.text: AutoGen (Microsoft) is a framework for building
. Its core model is that agents talk to each other. Each agent is a
that can send and receive messages. The framework manages the conversation: deciding who speaks next, when to stop, and whether a human should be consulted.
can execute code that the assistant writes, capture the output, and send it back as part of the conversation. This creates a natural
loop where the assistant writes code, the proxy runs it, and the assistant sees the result.
Base class; can send/receive messages and call tools
Pre-configured ConversableAgent with system prompt for coding/assistance
Represents a human; can execute code blocks from agent messages
The most common AutoGen pattern is a pair: an

that executes it. The proxy sends the initial task, the assistant responds with code, the proxy executes it, sends the result back, and the loop continues until the assistant produces a satisfactory final answer.

import os
from autogen import AssistantAgent, UserProxyAgent

# LLM config for Anthropic (AutoGen supports OpenAI-compatible endpoints)
# Note: AutoGen's native Anthropic integration requires autogen[anthropic]
llm_config = {
    "config_list": [
        {
            "model": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            "api_key": os.environ.get("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"),
            "api_type": "anthropic",
        }
    ],
    "temperature": 0.0,
}

assistant = AssistantAgent(
    name="assistant",
    llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message=(
        "You are a Python programmer. When asked to solve a problem, "
        "write Python code. After reviewing the output, say TERMINATE when done."
    ),
)

user_proxy = UserProxyAgent(
    name="user_proxy",
    human_input_mode="NEVER",   # fully automated, no human input
    max_consecutive_auto_reply=5,
    code_execution_config={
        "work_dir": "/tmp/autogen_workspace",
        "use_docker": False,    # set to True in production for isolation
    },
    is_termination_msg=lambda msg: "TERMINATE" in msg.get("content", ""),
)

# Start the conversation — the proxy sends the first message
user_proxy.initiate_chat(
    assistant,
    message="Write a Python function that checks if a number is prime. Test it for 17, 20, and 97.",
)
# Assistant writes the function and test code.
# Proxy executes it and sends back the output.
# Assistant reviews, adjusts if needed, and says TERMINATE when satisfied.
setting makes the proxy fully automated. Setting it to
prompts the human only when the assistant signals it is done — letting a human review before the conversation ends. Setting it to
requires human approval before every proxy reply. The
lambda checks each message for a termination signal and stops the conversation when it fires. The
is where the proxy writes and runs code — always isolate this directory from your project.
Not all AutoGen workflows need code execution. For agents that only generate text, plan tasks, or call APIs, disable code execution entirely to avoid accidental execution of model output.

from autogen import AssistantAgent, UserProxyAgent

# Assistant for content generation — no code execution
content_assistant = AssistantAgent(
    name="content_writer",
    llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message=(
        "You are a content writer. Write clear, factual text. "
        "Do not write code. Say TERMINATE when your writing is complete."
    ),
)

# Proxy with code execution disabled
no_code_proxy = UserProxyAgent(
    name="proxy",
    human_input_mode="NEVER",
    max_consecutive_auto_reply=3,
    code_execution_config=False,  # disables code execution entirely
    is_termination_msg=lambda msg: "TERMINATE" in msg.get("content", ""),
)

no_code_proxy.initiate_chat(
    content_assistant,
    message="Write a 3-sentence summary of what machine learning is.",
)
is the correct way to disable execution. Do not leave it at its default with a dummy
— the default may still execute code if the assistant produces a code block. Disable it explicitly for any assistant that should not run code.
runs a conversation among three or more agents. A
routes each message to the appropriate next speaker. By default, the manager selects the next speaker automatically (round-robin or LLM-based). You can constrain the selection with an

from autogen import AssistantAgent, UserProxyAgent, GroupChat, GroupChatManager

# Three specialist agents
planner = AssistantAgent(
    name="planner",
    llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message=(
        "You are a project planner. Break the task into steps and assign them. "
        "End your messages with 'Next: <agent_name>'."
    ),
)

coder = AssistantAgent(
    name="coder",
    llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message=(
        "You are a Python coder. Implement the plan. Write clean, working code. "
        "End your messages with 'Next: reviewer' after coding."
    ),
)

reviewer = AssistantAgent(
    name="reviewer",
    llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message=(
        "You are a code reviewer. Check for bugs and improvements. "
        "Say TERMINATE if the code is acceptable, otherwise say Next: coder."
    ),
)

proxy = UserProxyAgent(
    name="user",
    human_input_mode="NEVER",
    code_execution_config={"work_dir": "/tmp/autogen_ws", "use_docker": False},
    is_termination_msg=lambda msg: "TERMINATE" in msg.get("content", ""),
)

group_chat = GroupChat(
    agents=[proxy, planner, coder, reviewer],
    messages=[],
    max_round=12,
    speaker_selection_method="auto",  # LLM-based selection
)

manager = GroupChatManager(groupchat=group_chat, llm_config=llm_config)

proxy.initiate_chat(
    manager,
    message="Build a Python function that converts Celsius to Fahrenheit and includes unit tests.",
)
parameter caps the total number of turns across all agents.
uses the manager LLM to select the next speaker based on the conversation context. Setting it to
removes LLM-based selection and cycles through agents in fixed order — faster but less adaptive. The
initialization is required; the manager appends messages here so you can inspect the full conversation after the chat ends.
AutoGen agents can call Python functions directly — not just via generated code. Register a function with
on the assistant. The assistant sees the function as a tool it can invoke by name.

from autogen import AssistantAgent, UserProxyAgent, register_function
from typing import Annotated

# Tool implementation
def get_stock_price(ticker: Annotated[str, "Stock ticker symbol (e.g. AAPL)"]) -> str:
    """Get the current stock price for a ticker symbol."""
    # In production: call a real financial API
    prices = {"AAPL": 189.50, "GOOG": 175.20, "MSFT": 415.30}
    price = prices.get(ticker.upper())
    if price is None:
        return f"Ticker '{ticker}' not found."
    return f"{ticker.upper()}: ${price:.2f}"

assistant = AssistantAgent(
    name="stock_assistant",
    llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message=(
        "You are a financial assistant. Use the get_stock_price tool to look up prices. "
        "Say TERMINATE when done."
    ),
)

proxy = UserProxyAgent(
    name="user",
    human_input_mode="NEVER",
    code_execution_config=False,   # tools replace code execution
    is_termination_msg=lambda msg: "TERMINATE" in msg.get("content", ""),
)

# Register the function as a tool for both the assistant (declares) and proxy (executes)
register_function(
    get_stock_price,
    caller=assistant,    # assistant can call this tool
    executor=proxy,      # proxy executes it
    name="get_stock_price",
    description="Look up the current stock price for a ticker symbol.",
)

proxy.initiate_chat(
    assistant,
    message="What is the current price of Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT)?",
)
call wires the function to both agents: the
(assistant) sees it as a tool it can request, the
(proxy) actually runs the Python function when requested. The
type hint on the parameter becomes the tool's parameter description. With

, the proxy runs only registered tools — not arbitrary model-generated code. This is the recommended pattern for production agents where controlled tool invocation is preferable to free code execution.
Message Filtering and Custom Reply Functions
AutoGen agents process incoming messages through a chain of
. Each reply function can inspect the message and decide whether to respond. You can register custom reply functions to intercept messages, add preprocessing, or implement custom routing logic.

from autogen import ConversableAgent
from typing import Any

class FilteredAssistant(ConversableAgent):
    """
    An assistant that logs every message it receives
    and filters out messages that contain unsafe keywords.
    """
    BLOCKED_KEYWORDS = ["password", "secret", "private_key"]

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        # Register a custom reply function at the front of the chain
        self.register_reply(
            trigger=ConversableAgent,
            reply_func=self._safe_reply,
            position=0,  # check first before default handlers
        )

    def _safe_reply(
        self,
        messages: list | None = None,
        sender: ConversableAgent | None = None,
        config: Any = None,
    ) -> tuple[bool, str | None]:
        """
        Return (True, reply) to short-circuit the chain,
        or (False, None) to let the default handler run.
        """
        last = (messages or [{}])[-1]
        content = last.get("content", "") if isinstance(last, dict) else str(last)
        for kw in self.BLOCKED_KEYWORDS:
            if kw.lower() in content.lower():
                return True, f"I cannot process messages containing sensitive keywords."
        # No match — pass to default handler
        return False, None

filtered = FilteredAssistant(
    name="safe_assistant",
    llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message="You are a helpful assistant. Say TERMINATE when done.",
)

proxy = UserProxyAgent(
    name="user",
    human_input_mode="NEVER",
    code_execution_config=False,
    is_termination_msg=lambda msg: "TERMINATE" in msg.get("content", ""),
)

# Test: normal message
proxy.initiate_chat(filtered, message="What is the capital of France?")

# Test: blocked message
proxy.initiate_chat(filtered, message="What is the private_key format for RSA?")
# The _safe_reply fires and returns the blocked message without calling the LLM.
mechanism is AutoGen's extension point for custom behavior. The
parameter specifies which type of sender activates this reply function.
places it before the default LLM reply function, ensuring the filter runs first. Returning
short-circuits the chain: no other reply function runs, and the LLM is not called. Returning
passes control to the next reply function in the chain. This pattern lets you add logging, safety checks, caching, or routing without subclassing the agent beyond the
: a conversation between two agents that is initiated
a larger conversation. Use this to break a complex task into sub-tasks, each handled by a specialized pair of agents. The outer conversation calls
(plural) to run a sequence of inner conversations in order.

from autogen import AssistantAgent, UserProxyAgent

# Outer proxy orchestrates the sequence
orchestrator = UserProxyAgent(
    name="orchestrator",
    human_input_mode="NEVER",
    code_execution_config=False,
    is_termination_msg=lambda msg: "TERMINATE" in msg.get("content", ""),
)

# Specialist: research
research_agent = AssistantAgent(
    name="researcher",
    llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message=(
        "Research the requested topic thoroughly. "
        "Return key facts in a numbered list. Say TERMINATE when complete."
    ),
)

# Specialist: writing
writing_agent = AssistantAgent(
    name="writer",
    llm_config=llm_config,
    system_message=(
        "Take the research findings and write a 150-word summary. "
        "Say TERMINATE when complete."
    ),
)

# Sequence of two inner chats
chat_results = orchestrator.initiate_chats([
    {
        "recipient": research_agent,
        "message": "Research the environmental impact of electric vehicles.",
        "max_turns": 4,
        "summary_method": "last_msg",  # take the last message as the summary
    },
    {
        "recipient": writing_agent,
        "message": "Write a summary based on this research: {summary}",
        "max_turns": 2,
        "summary_method": "last_msg",
        "carryover": "The previous research found: {chat_result_0.summary}",
    },
])

print("Final output:", chat_results[-1].summary)
method runs each chat in the list sequentially. The
field injects output from a previous chat (
) into the next chat's initial message.
takes the last message in the conversation as the summary for passing to subsequent chats. Alternative:

, which prompts a model to summarize the entire conversation. Nested chats let you compose specialists without a monolithic group chat: each inner chat focuses on one well-defined sub-task, and the orchestrator stitches the outputs together.
agent-to-agent conversation as the primary model
. Agents exchange messages; the framework handles routing and execution. This differs from LangChain (one agent loop) and CrewAI (role-based team with a process).

: writing code, running it, observing the output, and iterating. The write-test-fix loop (assistant writes → proxy executes → assistant fixes) is its most natural application. For workflows without code execution — pure text processing, structured data pipelines — CrewAI or LangChain may be simpler. All three frameworks can be mixed: a CrewAI agent can call an AutoGen two-agent chat as a task, and LangChain tools work inside AutoGen agents.
AutoGen's core model is agents talking to each other.

The two-agent pattern (AssistantAgent + UserProxyAgent) handles code generation, execution, and review. Disable code execution and register explicit tools for safer, more predictable agents. Use GroupChat for workflows that need more than two agents. Control human involvement with human_input_mode: NEVER, TERMINATE, or ALWAYS. Set max_consecutive_auto_reply and max_round to prevent runaway conversations.
